label: Cracked or broken screen
descriptionShort: A cracked or unresponsive display is the most common fault and usually the most expensive single repair.
seoGuidance: Screen repair on the {device} is the single most common fix, and because the display is the priciest part its cost often decides the whole repair-or-replace question.

label: Battery (drains fast / won't hold charge)
descriptionShort: Battery capacity degrades with age; a replacement restores full-day use and is one of the highest-value repairs you can make.
seoGuidance: A fresh battery in the {device} is one of the cheapest ways to add years of life, so this repair almost always beats replacing the whole device.

label: Charging port (won't charge / loose cable)
descriptionShort: A worn or damaged charging port stops reliable charging; often it is just lint or a bent pin, so a diagnosis is worth it first.
seoGuidance: Before paying for a charging-port repair on the {device}, have a shop check for lint or a loose connector — the real fix is sometimes free.

label: Cracked back glass
descriptionShort: Back glass is cosmetic on most phones, but on modern Apple designs it is laser-bonded and surprisingly costly to replace officially.
seoGuidance: Cracked back glass on the {device} is mostly cosmetic, so weigh a costly official repair against simply using a case that hides it.

label: Camera (blurry / won't focus / black)
descriptionShort: Camera modules can fail or get scratched; replacements are modular on most phones and moderately priced.
seoGuidance: The {device} camera module is a self-contained part, so this repair is more predictable and often cheaper than people expect.

label: Water / liquid damage
descriptionShort: Liquid damage is unpredictable: costs depend entirely on what corroded. A diagnosis is essential before spending anything.
seoGuidance: Liquid damage on the {device} is the one fault where quotes are unreliable until a shop opens it up — get a diagnosis before deciding.

label: Speaker or microphone not working
descriptionShort: Audio faults are often a clogged mesh or a cheap modular part, making this one of the more affordable repairs.
seoGuidance: Speaker and microphone fixes on the {device} are usually inexpensive modular swaps, so repair almost always wins here.

label: Keyboard (sticky / dead keys)
descriptionShort: Laptop keyboards can fail from wear or spills; on some models the keyboard is fused to the top case, raising the cost sharply.
seoGuidance: Keyboard repair cost on the {device} swings widely depending on whether the key deck is a standalone part or fused to the chassis.

label: Broken or loose hinge
descriptionShort: A failing hinge can crack the lid or cables if ignored; parts are cheap but labour is fiddly.
seoGuidance: Fix a loose hinge on the {device} sooner rather than later — the part is cheap, but a neglected hinge can crack the screen and multiply the bill.

label: Storage / SSD failure
descriptionShort: On laptops with a socketed SSD this is a cheap DIY upgrade; on soldered-storage models it may mean a board-level repair.
seoGuidance: Whether an SSD fix on the {device} is a 15-minute DIY job or a board-level repair depends entirely on whether the storage is socketed or soldered.

label: Won't turn on / no power
descriptionShort: A dead device can be anything from a flat battery to a logic-board fault, so the cost range is wide until diagnosed.
seoGuidance: A {device} that won't power on could be a $0 fix or a logic-board job, so a free diagnosis is the only honest starting point.
